summary: Gruber reflects on Apple's 'It's Glowtime' event, praising the strong iPhone 16, Apple Watch Series 10, and AirPods 4 lineup, but confessing a sense of ennui about the predictability of it all. He draws a detailed contrast between Steve Jobs's Apple — quirky, risk-taking, occasionally reckless — and Tim Cook's Apple, which achieves utterly consistent excellence but rarely surprises. Gruber argues Cook hasn't missed any fundamental technology shift, but has eliminated the quirkiness and design gambling that made Apple thrilling to watch. He concludes that Cook's Apple is more financially successful and more consistently excellent, but less fun to obsess over, and acknowledges his dissatisfaction is ultimately selfish.
key_points: The iPhone 16, Apple Watch Series 10, and AirPods 4 represent Apple's three 'everyday carry' product families, all in excellent shape but evolving predictably.
Jobs was driven to improve how computers work; Cook is driven to improve how humans live — prioritizing accessibility, health, and environmental responsibility over design novelty.
Cook's Apple achieves near-perfection with tighter tolerances and fewer missteps, but at the cost of quirkiness, surprise, and the occasional thrilling failure.
No technology exists today that would enable another iPhone-level paradigm shift, regardless of who leads Apple.
The shift from live keynotes to pre-filmed events mirrors the broader change: more polished, more predictable, less exciting.
money_quotes: The obvious truth is that we all, including Apple, miss Steve Jobs.
Jobs was driven to improve the way computers work. Cook is driven to improve the way humans live.
Tim Cook's Apple doesn't make mistakes like that. That's ultimately why Cook's Apple is more successful.
The delight is still there, but there's less amazement.
Cook has patience where Jobs would grow restless.
Apple today is aiming for, and achieving, utterly consistent excellence. Quirkiness no longer fits.
Cook's Apple is not overly cautious, but it's never reckless. Jobs's Apple was occasionally reckless, for better and worse.
Sometimes imperfections are what we love most about products. Or people.
key_insight: Tim Cook's Apple has traded the exhilarating peaks and embarrassing valleys of the Jobs era for utterly consistent excellence — a better business outcome, but a less thrilling one to witness.
